steps.title: Identify the controlling contrast
steps.content: Hawthorne directly compares historical knowledge (from historians) to a map: accurate but cold. He contrasts this with what he wishes it resembled—a landscape painting: vivid and charming. This contrast explains why historical figures remain mere names rather than feeling like real people.

steps.title: Link the contrast to the main idea
steps.content: B correctly identifies Hawthorne's argument: historical figures are documented as facts and records rather than as vivid representations of their personalities. Choices A, C, and D either focus on the scope of subjects, misread the fireside-legend reference, or discuss map utility. That matches choice B — B is correct.
